title: End of module: Knowledge Representation and Reasoning

questions.text: In predicate notation, what does the fact `has_feathers(tweety)` mean?
choices: Tweety is a type of feather
questions.explanation: A fact is written `predicate(object)`, so `has_feathers(tweety)` reads as "tweety has feathers" — the predicate names the property, the argument names the object it holds for.

questions.text: In the rule `flies(X) :- bird(X), not(penguin(X))`, what does `X` represent?
choices: A variable that stands for any object
questions.explanation: `X` is a variable. To use the rule for a particular object you substitute (ground) that object for `X` throughout the rule — the same rule works for tweety, percy, or any other object.

choices: at least one condition in its body is true
every condition in its body is known to be true, for the same substitution
its conclusion is already a known fact
it appears first in the list of rules
questions.explanation: A rule's body is a conjunction (AND) of conditions — a single unmet condition blocks the whole rule. Only when every condition holds does the rule fire and its conclusion get derived.

questions.text: Using `predicate(object)` notation, write the fact "Percy is a penguin".
questions.explanation: The predicate `penguin` applied to the object `percy` gives `penguin(percy)`, exactly mirroring `bird(tweety)` for "Tweety is a bird".

questions.text: In the Tweety/Percy example, why does the knowledge base fail to derive `flies(percy)`?
choices: Because `bird(percy)` is not a known fact
Because `penguin(percy)` is a known fact, so `NOT penguin(percy)` is false
Because forward chaining was not used
questions.explanation: `bird(percy)` is true, but the rule also requires `NOT penguin(percy)`. Since `penguin(percy)` is a known fact, that condition is false, the rule's body is not entirely satisfied, and the rule does not fire.

choices: Goal-driven: start from the question and work backward
Data-driven: start from known facts and apply rules to derive new ones
A random search over all possible facts
A method that only works with numeric facts
questions.explanation: Forward chaining is data-driven: it starts with the facts you have and repeatedly fires any rule whose body is fully satisfied, growing the set of known facts until a fixpoint (or a chosen goal) is reached.

questions.text: In the farmyard knowledge base (rules R1–R3: `has_feathers(X), lays_eggs(X) -> bird(X)`; `bird(X), can_swim(X) -> waterbird(X)`; `waterbird(X), has_webbed_feet(X), quacks(X) -> duck(X)`), list, in order, the three new facts forward chaining derives about kim up to and including `duck(kim)`. Separate them with commas, e.g. "fact1, fact2, fact3".
questions.explanation: R1 fires first (its body `has_feathers(kim), lays_eggs(kim)` is already known), deriving `bird(kim)`. That lets R2 fire, deriving `waterbird(kim)`. That lets R3 fire, deriving `duck(kim)`. Order: `bird(kim)`, `waterbird(kim)`, `duck(kim)`.

questions.text: Starting from the initial facts about kim and max, how many new facts in total does forward chaining derive from the 5-rule farmyard knowledge base (R1–R5) before it reaches a fixpoint (no more rules can fire)?
questions.explanation: Forward chaining derives `bird(kim)`, `waterbird(kim)`, `duck(kim)`, `mammal(max)`, `carnivore(max)` — five new facts in total — before no rule can fire any further.

questions.text: Which of the following facts does forward chaining derive when run to a fixpoint on the farmyard knowledge base (kim and max, rules R1–R5)?
questions.explanation: Forward chaining derives `bird(kim)`, `waterbird(kim)`, `duck(kim)`, `mammal(max)` and `carnivore(max)`. There is no rule in the knowledge base that concludes `reptile(kim)`, so it is never derived.

questions.text: When backward chaining proves `duck(kim)` in the farmyard example, which rule is consulted first?
questions.explanation: Backward chaining starts from the goal `duck(kim)` and looks for a rule whose head matches it — that is R3. Only afterwards, to prove R3's `waterbird(kim)` subgoal, does it consult R2, then R1.

questions.text: Which fact is never consulted while backward chaining proves `duck(kim)` in the farmyard example?
questions.explanation: Proving `duck(kim)` only ever needs facts about kim (`has_feathers`, `lays_eggs`, `can_swim`, `has_webbed_feet`, `quacks`). `gives_milk(max)` belongs to the unrelated mammal/carnivore rules (R4, R5) and backward chaining never looks at it.

questions.text: Which reasoning strategy is generally more efficient for answering one specific, known question in a large knowledge base with many irrelevant rules?
choices: Forward chaining, because it derives everything the rules allow
Backward chaining, because it only explores what the goal depends on
Neither — both always explore the entire knowledge base
It depends only on how many facts there are, never on the rules
questions.explanation: Backward chaining works only on the branch of rules and facts the goal actually depends on, so in a large knowledge base with many irrelevant rules it typically does far less work than forward chaining, which derives every consequence of the facts regardless of whether it is asked for.
